volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: Amphitryon (Section 1.1.270 - Section 1.1.344c)

sections.label: Section 1.1.270
sections.original: sed quid illúc est ? caelum aspectat . observabo quam rem agat .
sections.translation: But what means this ? He is looking up at the sky . I ’ ll watch what scheme he ’ s about .

sections.label: Section 1.1.271
sections.original: Certe edepol , si quicquamst aliud quod credam aut certo sciam ,
sections.translation: Upon my faith , for sure , if there is aught besides that I believe , or know for certain , I do believe that this night the God of Night [*] has gone to sleep drunk ; for neither does the Wain move itself in any direction in the sky , nor does the Moon bestir herself anywhere from where she first arose ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.275
sections.original: nec Iugulae neque Vesperugo neque Vergiliae óccidunt .
sections.translation: nor does Orion [*] , or the Evening Star [*] , or the Pleiades , set . In such a fashion are the stars standing stock-still , and the night is yielding not a jot to the day .

sections.label: Section 1.1.277
sections.original: Perge , Nox , ut occepisti , gere patri morem meo :
sections.translation: Go on , Night , as you ’ ve begun , and pay obedience to my father . In best style [*] , the best of services are you performing for the best of beings ; in giving this , you reap a fair return .

sections.label: Section 1.1.279
sections.original: Neque ego hac nocte longiorem me vidisse censeo ,
sections.translation: I do not think that I have ever seen a longer night than this ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.280
sections.original: nisi item unam , verberatus quam pependi perpetem ;
sections.translation: except one of like fashion , which livelong night I was hanging up , having been first whipped . Even that as well , by my troth , does this one by far exceed in its length . I ’ faith , I really do believe that the Sun ’ s asleep , and is thoroughly drenched . It ’ s a wonder to me if he hasn ’ t indulged himself a little too much at dinner .

sections.label: Section 1.1.284
sections.original: Ain vero , verbero ? deos esse tui similis putas ?
sections.translation: Do you really say so , you scoundrel ? Do you think that the Gods are like yourself ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.285
sections.original: ego pol te istis tuis pro dictis et male factis , furcifer ,
sections.translation: I ’ faith , you hang-dog , I ’ ll entertain you for these speeches and misdeeds of yours ; only come this way , will you , and you ’ ll find your ruin .

sections.label: Section 1.1.287
sections.original: Vbi sunt isti scortatores , qui soli inviti cubant ?
sections.translation: Where are those wenchers , who unwillingly lie a-bed alone ? A rare night this for making the best of what was a bad bargain at first [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.1.289
sections.original: Meus pater nunc pro huius verbis recte et sapienter facit ,
sections.translation: My father then , according to this fellow ’ s words , is doing rightly and wisely , who in his amorousness , indulging his passion , is lying in the embraces of Alcmena .

sections.label: Section 1.1.291
sections.original: Ibo ut erus quod imperavit Alcumenae nuntiem .
sections.translation: I ’ ll go tell Alcmena , as my master ordered me . ( Advancing , he discovers MERCURY .) But who is this fellow that I see before the house at this time of night ? I don ’ t like it .

sections.label: Section 1.1.293
sections.original: Nullust hoc metuculosus aeque .
sections.translation: There is not in existence another such cowardly fellow as this .

sections.label: Section 1.1.293b
sections.original: Mi in mentem venit ,
sections.translation: Now , when I think of it , this fellow wishes to take my mantle off once more [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.1.295
sections.original: Timet homo : deludam ego illum .
sections.translation: The fellow ’ s afraid ; I ’ ll have some sport with him .

sections.label: Section 1.1.295b
sections.original: Perii , dentes pruriunt ;
sections.translation: I ’ m quite undone , my teeth are chattering . For sure , on my arrival , he is about to receive me with the hospitality of his fist . He ’ s a merciful person , I suppose ; now , because my master has obliged me to keep awake , with his fists just now he ’ ll be making me go to sleep . I ’ m most confoundedly undone . Troth now , prithee , look , how big and how strong he is .

sections.label: Section 1.1.300
sections.original: Clare advorsum fabulabor , ut hic auscultet quae loquar ;
sections.translation: I ’ ll talk at him aloud , he shall hear what I say . Therefore indeed , in a still greater degree , shall he conceive fears within himself . ( In a loud voice , holding up his fists .) Come , fists , it ’ s a long time now since you found provision for my stomach ; it seems to have taken place quite a long time ago , when yesterday you laid four men asleep , stript naked .

sections.label: Section 1.1.304b
sections.original: Formido male ,
sections.translation: I ’ m dreadfully afraid

sections.label: Section 1.1.305
sections.original: né ego hic nomen meum commutem et Quintus fiam e Sosia ;
sections.translation: lest I should be changing my name here , and become a Quintus [*] instead of a Sosia . He declares that he has laid four men asleep ; I fear lest I should be adding to that number .

sections.label: Section 1.1.307b
sections.original: em , nunciam ergo : sic volo .
sections.translation: Well , now then for it . This is the way I intend .

sections.label: Section 1.1.308
sections.original: Cingitur : certe expedit se .
sections.translation: He is girded tight ; for sure , he ’ s getting himself ready .

sections.label: Section 1.1.308b
sections.original: Non feret quin vapulet .
sections.translation: He shan ’ t get off without getting a thrashing .

sections.label: Section 1.1.309
sections.original: Quis homo ?
sections.translation: What person , I wonder ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.309b
sections.original: Quisquis homo huc profecto venerit , pugnos edet .
sections.translation: Beyond a doubt , whatever person comes this way , he shall eat my fists .

sections.label: Section 1.1.310
sections.original: Apage , non placet me hoc noctis esse : cenavi modo ;
sections.translation: Get out with you , I don ’ t wish to eat at this time of night ; I ’ ve lately dined . Therefore do you , if you are wise , bestow your dinner on those who are hungry .

sections.label: Section 1.1.312
sections.original: Haud malum huic est pondus pugno .
sections.translation: The weight of this fist is no poor one .

sections.label: Section 1.1.312b
sections.original: Perii , pugnos ponderat .
sections.translation: I ’ m done for ; he is poising his fists .

sections.label: Section 1.1.313
sections.original: Quid si ego illum tractim tangam , ut dormiat ?
sections.translation: What if I were to touch him , stroking him down [*] , so that he may go to sleep ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.313b
sections.original: Servaveris ,
sections.translation: You would be proving my salvation ; for I ’ ve been watching most confoundedly these three nights running [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.1.314b
sections.original: Pessumest ,
sections.translation: My hand refuses to learn to strike his cheek ; it cannot do a disgraceful action .

sections.label: Section 1.1.316
sections.original: alia forma ésse oportet quem tu pugno legeris .
sections.translation: Hand of mine , of a changed form must he become whom you smite with this fist .

sections.label: Section 1.1.317
sections.original: Illic homo me interpolabit meumque os finget denuo .
sections.translation: This fellow will be furbishing me up , and be moulding my face anew .

sections.label: Section 1.1.318
sections.original: Exossatum os esse oportet quem probe percusseris .
sections.translation: The man that you hit full , his face must surely be boned .

sections.label: Section 1.1.319
sections.original: Mirum ni hic me quasi murenam éxossare cogitat .
sections.translation: It ’ s a wonder if this fellow isn ’ t thinking of boning me just like a lamprey . Away with a fellow that bones people ! If he sees me , I ’ m a dead man .

sections.label: Section 1.1.321
sections.original: Olet homo quidam malo suo .
sections.translation: Some fellow is stinking to his destruction .

sections.label: Section 1.1.321b
sections.original: ei , numnam ego óbolui ?
sections.translation: Woe to me ! Is it I that stink ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.322
sections.original: Atque haud longe abesse oportet , verum longe hinc afuit .
sections.translation: And he cannot be very far off ; but he has been a long way off from here .

sections.label: Section 1.1.323
sections.original: Illic homo superstitiosust .
sections.translation: This person ’ s a wizard [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.1.323b
sections.original: Gestiunt pugni mihi .
sections.translation: My fists are longing .

sections.label: Section 1.1.324
sections.original: Si in me exercituru'sexerciturus es , quaeso in parietem ut primum domes .
sections.translation: If you are going to exercise them upon me , I beg that you ’ ll first cool them down against the wall .

sections.label: Section 1.1.325
sections.original: Vox mi ad aures advolavit .
sections.translation: A voice has come flying to my ears .

sections.label: Section 1.1.325b
sections.original: Né ego homo infelix fui ,
sections.translation: Unlucky fellow , for sure , was I , who didn ’ t clip its wings . I ’ ve got a voice with wings , it seems .

sections.label: Section 1.1.327
sections.original: Illic homo a me sibi malam rem arcessit iumento suo .
sections.translation: This fellow is demanding of me for himself a heavy punishment for his beast ’ s back [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.1.328
sections.original: Non equidem ullum habeo iumentum .
sections.translation: As for me , I ’ ve got no beast ’ s back .

sections.label: Section 1.1.328b
sections.original: Onerandus est pugnis probe .
sections.translation: He must be well loaded with my fists .

sections.label: Section 1.1.329
sections.original: Lassus sum hercle , navi ut vectus huc sum : etiam nunc nauseo ;
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I ’ m fatigued , coming from board ship , when I was brought hither ; even now I ’ m sea-sick .

sections.label: Section 1.1.330
sections.original: vix incedo inanis , ne ire posse cum onere existimes .
sections.translation: Without a burden , I can hardly creep along , so don ’ t think that with a load I can go .

sections.label: Section 1.1.331
sections.original: Certe enim hic nescio quis loquitur .
sections.translation: Why , surely , somebody [*] is speaking here .

sections.label: Section 1.1.331b
sections.original: Salvos sum , non me videt :
sections.translation: I ’ m all right , he doesn ’ t see me ; he thinks it ’ s Somebody speaking : Sosia is certainly my name .

sections.label: Section 1.1.333
sections.original: Hinc enim míhi dextra vox auris , ut videtur , verberat .
sections.translation: But here , from the right-hand side , the voice , as it seems , strikes upon my ear .

sections.label: Section 1.1.334
sections.original: Metuo , vocis ne vicem hodie hic vapulem , quae hunc verberat .
sections.translation: I ’ m afraid that I shall be getting a thrashing here this day , in place of my voice , that ’ s striking him .

sections.label: Section 1.1.335
sections.original: Optume eccum incedit ad me .
sections.translation: Here he is — he ’ s coming towards me , most opportunely .

sections.label: Section 1.1.335b
sections.original: Timeo , totus torpeo .
sections.translation: I ’ m terrified — I ’ m numbed all over . Upon my faith , I don ’ t know where in the world I now am , if any one should ask me ; and to my misfortune , I cannot move myself for fright . It ’ s all up with me ; the orders of his master and Sosia are lost together . But I ’ m determined boldly to address this fellow to his face ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.340
sections.original: qui possim videri huic fortis , a me ut abstineat manum .
sections.translation: so that I may be able to appear valiant to him ; that he may keep his hands off me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.341
sections.original: Quo ambulas tu , qui Volcanum in cornu conclusum geris ?
sections.translation: Where are you going , you that are carrying Vulcan enclosed in your horn [*] ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.342
sections.original: Quid id exquiris tu , qui pugnis os exossas hominibus ?
sections.translation: Why do you make that enquiry , you who are boning men ’ s heads with your fists ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.343
sections.original: Servosne es an liber ?
sections.translation: Are you slave or free man ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.343b
sections.original: Vtcumque animo conlibitum est meo .
sections.translation: Just as it suits my inclination .

sections.label: Section 1.1.344
sections.original: Ain vero ?
sections.translation: Do you really say so ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.344b
sections.original: Aio enim vero .
sections.translation: I really do say so .

sections.label: Section 1.1.344c
sections.original: Verbero .
sections.translation: Whip-scoundrel [*] !
